The general colour of the upper parts of the adult bird is bright metallic blue. The head has a short crest of black and blue feathers, which gives rise to the scientific name. The face, cheeks, and underparts are rufous, and there are white patches on the throat and rear neck sides. The bill is black in young birds and reddish-orange in adults. It feeds on small fish, crustaceans, and aquatic insects.

The European Bee-eater (Merops apiaster) is a near passerine bird in the bee-eater family, Meropidae. It breeds in southern Europe and in parts of North Africa and western Asia. It is strongly migratory, wintering in tropical Africa. This species occurs as a spring overshoot north of its range, with occasional breeding in northwest Europe. It is a richly coloured, slender bird. It has brown and yellow upperparts, whilst the wings are green and the beak is black. It can reach a length of 27–29 cm (10.6–11.4 in), including the two elongated central tail feathers. As the name suggests, bee-eaters predominantly eat insects, especially bees, wasps, and hornets. They catch insects in flight, in sorties from an open perch.

The Horned Puffin (Fratercula corniculata) is an auk, similar in appearance to the Atlantic Puffin; its bill is yellow at the base and red at the tip. It is a pelagic seabird that feeds primarily by diving for fish. It nests in colonies, often with other auks. It is found in the North Pacific Ocean, including the coasts of Alaska, Siberia, and British Columbia. The name 'horned' comes from a small black fleshy 'horn' found above the eyes of adult birds in the breeding season. Like other puffins, it has a large, brightly colored bill during the breeding season which it sheds in the winter. They are social birds and spend most of their time at sea when not breeding.

The Red-headed Woodpecker (Melanerpes erythrocephalus) is a small or medium-sized woodpecker from temperate North America. Their breeding habitat is open country across southern Canada and the eastern-central United States. Adults are very strikingly tri-colored, with a black back and tail and a white belly and rump. Their head and neck are bright crimson red. Their wings are black with white secondary feathers. Adult males and females are identical in plumage. Juveniles have very similar markings, but have greyish brown heads. These woodpeckers are known for their unusual habit of caching food, such as nuts and insects, in crevices and cracks in trees. They are also highly aerial and frequently catch insects in mid-air.

The Tufted Titmouse (Baeolophus bicolor) is a small songbird from North America, a species in the tit and chickadee family (Paridae). The black-crested titmouse, found from central and southern Texas southwards, was included as a subspecies but is now considered a separate species, Baeolophus atricristatus. These birds have grey upperparts and white underparts with a white face, a grey crest, a dark forehead, and a short stout bill. They are common in deciduous and mixed forests as well as gardens and parks in the eastern United States. Tufted Titmice are social birds and often join mixed-species foraging flocks. They eat a variety of insects, seeds, and berries.

The Swallow-tailed Kite (Elanoides forficatus) is a perptiline bird of prey which breeds from the southeastern United States to eastern Peru and northern Argentina. It is the only species in the genus Elanoides. Most North and Central American breeders winter in South America where the species is resident year-round. This is a very graceful bird with a deeply forked tail, making it one of the most distinctive raptors in flight. The plumage is a striking contrast of black and white. They are often seen soaring over wetlands and forests, catching insects, small reptiles, and amphibians in mid-air or plucking them from the canopy. They are highly social, especially during migration and around nesting sites.

The Greater Roadrunner (Geococcyx californianus) is a long-legged bird in the cuckoo family, Cuculidae, from the Southwestern United States and Mexico. The Latin name means 'Californian Earth-cuckoo'. Along with the lesser roadrunner, it is one of two species in the roadrunner genus Geococcyx. This roadrunner is also known as the chaparral cock, ground cuckoo, and snake killer. It is a large, slender, black-brown and white-streaked ground bird with a distinctive crest and a long tail. It is famous for its speed and ability to kill rattlesnakes. While it can fly, it spends most of its time on the ground and can run at speeds of up to 32 km/h (20 mph). It inhabits arid and semi-arid regions.

The Indian Peafowl (Pavo cristatus), also known as the Common Peafowl, and Blue Peafowl, is a large and brightly coloured bird, is a species of peafowl native to the Indian subcontinent, but introduced to many other parts of the world. The male peafowl is predominantly blue with a fan-like crest of spatula-tipped wire-like feathers and is best known for the long train made up of elongated upper-tail covert feathers which bear colourful eyespots. These stiff feathers are raised into a fan and quivered in a display during courtship. Despite the length and size of these covert feathers, peacocks are still capable of flight. Peahens lack the train, and have a greenish lower neck and duller brown plumage.

The Common Loon (Gavia immer), also known as the Great Northern Diver, is a large member of the loon, or diver, family of birds. Adults can range from 61–100 cm (24–39 in) in length with a 122–152 cm (48–60 in) wingspan. The species is found across North America and parts of Europe and Asia. In the breeding season, adults have a black head, white underparts, and a checkered black-and-white mantle. Non-breeding plumage is brownish-grey. Their bill is black-blue and held horizontally. Common Loons are famous for their haunting, yodel-like calls heard on northern lakes. They are excellent swimmers and divers, often staying underwater for long periods while hunting fish. They are the state bird of Minnesota and appear on the Canadian one-dollar coin.

The Pileated Woodpecker (Dryocopus pileatus) is a large, mostly black woodpecker native to North America. Inhabiting deciduous forests in eastern North America, the Great Lakes, the boreal forests of Canada, and parts of the Pacific Coast, it is the largest woodpecker in the United States, following the probably extinct ivory-billed woodpecker. It is about 40 to 49 cm (16 to 19 in) long and weighs 250 to 400 g (8.8 to 14.1 oz). It is characterized by its large size and its bright red crest. The name 'pileated' refers to the bird's prominent crest, from the Latin pileatus meaning 'capped'. They are known for digging large, rectangular holes in trees while searching for carpenter ants and other insects.

The House Sparrow (Passer domesticus) is a bird of the sparrow family Passeridae, found in most parts of the world. It is a small bird that has a typical length of 16 cm (6.3 in) and a mass of 24–39.5 g (0.85–1.39 oz). Females and young birds are coloured pale brown and grey, and males have brighter black, white, and brown markings. One of about 25 species in the genus Passer, the house sparrow is native to most of Europe, the Mediterranean Basin, and a large part of Asia. Its intentional or accidental introduction to many regions, including parts of Australasia, Africa, and the Americas, makes it the most widely distributed wild bird. The House Sparrow is strongly associated with human habitation and can thrive in urban or rural settings.

The Cedar Waxwing (Bombycilla cedrorum) is a member of the waxwing family of passerine birds. It is a medium-sized bird, approximately 15–18 cm (5.9–7.1 in) long and weighing about 30 g (1.1 oz). Its name comes from its appetite for the cones of the eastern red cedar and the red waxy secretions on the tips of its wing feathers. The plumage is a blend of brown, gray, and yellow. It has a distinctive crest and an orange or yellow-tipped tail. Cedar Waxwings are highly social birds and are often seen in large, noisy flocks. They are primary fruit eaters, although they also eat insects during the breeding season. They are found across North America, breeding in open woodlands and wintering further south into Central America.

Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) is a medium-sized hummingbird native to the west coast of North America. It was named after Anna Masséna, Duchess of Rivoli. In the early 20th century, Anna's hummingbird bred only in northern Baja California and southern California. The transplanting of exotic ornamental plants in residential areas throughout the Pacific coast and inland deserts allowed the species to expand its breeding range. Adult males have a brilliant iridescent reddish-pink throat and crown, while females are primarily green with some spotted gray underparts. Using their long tongues, they consume nectar from flowers and also catch small insects. They are known for their spectacular courtship dives by the males.

The Black-capped Chickadee (Poecile atricapillus) is a small, North American songbird, a passerine bird in the tit family Paridae. It is the state bird of Massachusetts and Maine in the United States, and the provincial bird of New Brunswick in Canada. It is notable for its capacity to lower its body temperature during cold winter nights and its good memory for where it has stored food. These birds have a black cap and bib with white cheeks, a grey back, and buffy flanks. They are common in deciduous and mixed forests, as well as suburban gardens. Chickadees are very vocal and have a complex range of calls, the most famous being the 'chick-a-dee-dee-dee' which they use to communicate with their group.

The American Goldfinch (Spinus tristis) is a small North American bird in the finch family. It is migratory, ranging from mid-Alberta to North Carolina during the breeding season, and from just south of the Canada–United States border to Mexico during the winter. The only finch in its subfamily to undergo a complete molt, the American goldfinch displays sexual dimorphism in its coloration; the male is a vibrant yellow in the summer and an olive color during the winter, while the female is a dull yellow-brown shade year-round that brightens only slightly during the summer. The American Goldfinch is a granivore and adapted for the consumption of seedheads, with a conical beak to remove the seeds and agile feet to grip the stems of seedheads while feeding.

The Common Starling (Sturnus vulgaris), also known as the European Starling or simply the Starling in the British Isles, is a medium-sized passerine bird in the starling family, Sturnidae. It is about 20 cm (8 in) long and has glossy black plumage with a metallic sheen, which is speckled with white at some times of year. The legs are pink and the bill is black in winter and yellow in summer; young birds have browner plumage than the adults. It is a noisy bird, being a highly social species with a wide variety of both melodic and mechanical-sounding calls. Its gift for mimicry has been noted in literature since antiquity. The common starling has about 12 subspecies breeding in temperate habitats across its native range in temperate Europe and western Asia.

The Barn Swallow (Hirundo rustica) is the most widespread species of swallow in the world. It is a distinctive passerine bird with blue upperparts and a long, deeply forked tail. It is found in Europe, Asia, Africa and the Americas. In the Anglosphere it is known as the barn swallow; in northern Europe it is just called the swallow. It is the most common swallow in the world with a huge range. These birds are highly aerial and spend most of their time flying, catching insects on the wing. They build cup-shaped nests from mud pellets in caves or buildings (hence the name). Barn swallows are migratory, breeding in the Northern Hemisphere and wintering in the Southern Hemisphere. They are often associated with rural areas and farms.

The Mountain Bluebird (Sialia currucoides) is a medium-sized bird weighing about 30 g (1.1 oz) with a length from 15–20 cm (5.9–7.9 in). They have light underparts and black eyes. Adult males have thin bills and are bright turquoise-blue and somewhat lighter underneath. Adult females have duller blue wings and tail, grey breast, grey crown, throat and back. In breeding season, the males' plumage is particularly vibrant. The Mountain Bluebird is migratory, found in western North America. They inhabit open country, including grasslands and sagebrush, often at higher elevations. They feed on insects and berries. They are secondary cavity nesters, meaning they use holes in trees that were previously made by woodpeckers or other animals.

The Eastern Screech-Owl (Megascops asio) is a small owl that is relatively common in Eastern North America, from Mexico to Canada. It is mostly nocturnal, although it is sometimes active at dusk and dawn. This species is native to most grassy or wooded environments, and has adapted well to man-made development, although it avoids intensive agriculture. Eastern Screech-Owls are actually quite small, only 16 to 25 cm (6.3 to 9.8 in) tall. They come in two color morphs: gray and rufous (reddish-brown). Both morphs have complex patterns of streaks and bars that provide excellent camouflage against tree bark. Like many owls, they have 'ear' tufts which are actually just feathers and not related to their hearing. They hunt a variety of insects and small mammals.

The Ovenbird (Seiurus aurocapilla) is a small songbird of the New World warbler family (Parulidae). This bird is common and widespread in North America, breeding in deciduous and mixed forests. It is an unusual warbler because it spends most of its time on the forest floor rather than in the trees. The Ovenbird gets its name from its unique nest, which is built on the ground and covered with a dome of leaves and grass, making it look like a Dutch oven. The bird is olive-brown above and white with black streaks below, with an orange patch on its crown bordered by black. They are known for their loud, ringing 'teacher-teacher-teacher' song. They are migratory, wintering in Florida, Central America, and the Caribbean.

The Magnificent Frigatebird (Fregata magnificens) is a seabird in the frigatebird family Fregatidae. With a length of 89–114 cm (35–45 in) and a wingspan of 217–244 cm (85–96 in), it is the largest species of frigatebird. It occurs over tropical and subtropical waters off America, between northern Mexico and Peru on the Pacific coast and between Florida and southern Brazil on the Atlantic coast. It also breeds in the Cape Verde Islands. This bird is all black except for a white patch on the breast of the female and immature birds. The male has a spectacular red throat pouch (gular sac) which it inflates during the breeding season to attract mates. They are agile fliers and are known for kleptoparasitism, stealing food from other seabirds.

The Brown Pelican (Pelecanus occidentalis) is a bird of the pelican family, Pelecanidae, one of the three species found in the Americas and one of two that feed by diving in water. It is found on the Atlantic Coast from New Jersey and the mouth of the Amazon River, and on the Pacific Coast from British Columbia to northern Chile, including the Galápagos Islands. The Brown Pelican is a large bird with a long bill and a large throat pouch used for catching fish. It is primarily brown and grey, with a white head and neck in the non-breeding season. They are famous for their dramatic plunge-dives from high in the air to catch fish near the surface. They are social birds and often nest in large colonies on islands.

The Common Eider (Somateria mollissima) is a large sea-duck that is distributed over the northern coasts of Europe, North America and eastern Siberia. It breeds in Arctic and some northern temperate regions, but winters somewhat farther south in temperate zones, when it can form sizeable flocks on coastal waters. It can fly at speeds up to 113 km/h (70 mph). The eider's nest is built close to the sea and is lined with eiderdown, plucked from the female's breast. This soft and warm lining has long been harvested for filling pillows and quilts, but in more recent years, this has been largely replaced by down from domestic farm-geese and synthetic alternatives. Male eiders have a striking black and white plumage, while females are brown and barred.

Wilson's Snipe (Gallinago delicata) is a small, stocky shorebird in the family Scolopacidae. It was formerly considered a subspecies of the Common Snipe (G. gallinago). It is found in North America and breeds in marshes, bogs, and wet meadows across Canada and the northern United States. It is a migratory bird, wintering in the southern United States and Central America. The bird is mottled brown, black, and white with a very long bill used for probing the mud for worms and other invertebrates. They are known for their secretive nature and their 'winnowing' display flight during the breeding season, which produces a ghostly, whistling sound as air passes through their tail feathers. They are often seen in wet, marshy areas.

The Killdeer (Charadrius vociferus) is a medium-sized plover. The genus name Charadrius is a Late Latin word for a yellowish bird mentioned in the fourth-century Vulgate. It derives from Ancient Greek kharadrios a bird found in ravines and river valleys. The specific vociferus is Latin and means 'noisy, clamorous'. The Killdeer is a common and widespread bird throughout North America. It is easily identified by its two black breast bands and its loud, repetitive 'kill-dee' call. One of its most famous behaviors is the 'broken-wing act', where an adult will pretend to have a broken wing to lure predators away from its nest or chicks. Killdeers are often found in open areas such as fields, lawns, and parking lots as well as near water.

The Trumpeter Swan (Cygnus buccinator) is a species of swan native to North America. It is the heaviest living bird native to North America and is the largest extant species of waterfowl in the world, with a wingspan that can exceed 3 m (10 ft). It is the North American counterpart of the Whooper Swan of Eurasia. By 1933, fewer than 70 wild trumpeter swans were known to exist, and extinction seemed nearly certain. However, aerial surveys discovered a large population in Alaska, and conservation efforts have since helped the population recover to over 46,000 birds. The Trumpeter Swan is all white with a black bill and black feet. Its name comes from its loud, trumpet-like call. They inhabit large, shallow ponds and wide, slow-moving rivers.

The Canada Goose (Branta canadensis) is a large wild goose with a black head and neck, white cheeks, white under its chin, and a brown body. Native to arctic and temperate regions of North America, its migration occasionally reaches northern Europe. It has been introduced to the United Kingdom, Ireland, New Zealand, Argentina, Chile, and the Falkland Islands. Like most geese, the Canada goose is primarily herbivorous and normally migratory; it tends to be found on or near fresh water. This species is highly adaptable and has thrived in human-altered landscapes, often being seen in parks, golf courses, and suburban areas. They are known for their V-shaped flight formations during migration and their loud honking. They are very protective of their nests and young.

The Wild Turkey (Meleagris gallopavo) is an upland ground bird native to North America and is the ancestor of the domestic turkey, which was originally derived from a southern Mexican subspecies of wild turkey. It is one of the two extant species of turkey. Adults are large birds with long, reddish-yellow to grey-green legs. Their feathers are generally dark brown, appearing iridescent with shades of green, copper, and bronze. Males, called toms or gobblers, have a large, fan-like tail and a fleshy protuberance called a snood that hangs from the beak. They are known for their 'gobble' call. Wild turkeys inhabit forests with openings and are capable of short, fast flights, although they spend most of their time on the ground.

The Greater Sage-Grouse (Centrocercus urophasianus), also known as the sagehen, is the largest grouse in North America. Its range is sagebrush country in the western United States and southern Alberta and Saskatchewan, Canada. It was known as simply the Sage Grouse until the Gunnison sage-grouse was recognized as a separate species in 2000. The Mono Basin population of sage grouse may also be distinct. Adults have a long, pointed tail and legs with feathers to the toes. The adult male has a yellow patch over each eye, is greyish on top with a white breast, and has a dark brown throat and a black belly; two yellowish sacs on the neck are inflated during courtship display. They are famous for their elaborate 'lek' displays where males gather to compete for mates.

The Northern Gannet (Morus bassanus) is a seabird, the largest member of the gannet family, Sulidae. It is native to the coasts of the Atlantic Ocean, breeding in Western Europe and Northeastern North America. It is a large bird, with a length of 87–100 cm (34–39 in) and a wingspan of 165–180 cm (65–71 in). The plumage is primarily white with black wingtips. The head and neck are tinged with yellow. The bill is long and pointed. Northern Gannets are famous for their dramatic plunge-dives into the ocean to catch fish, reaching speeds of up to 100 km/h (62 mph). They nest in large, noisy colonies on coastal cliffs and islands. Most populations are migratory, wintering at sea further south in the Atlantic.

The Double-crested Cormorant (Nannopterum auritum) is a member of the cormorant family of seabirds. Its habitat is near rivers and lakes as well as in coastal areas, and is widely distributed across North America, from the Aleutian Islands in Alaska down to Florida and Mexico. Measuring 70–90 cm (28–35 in) in length, it is an all-black bird that gains a small double crest of black and white feathers in the breeding season. It has a bare patch of orange-yellow facial skin. Like other cormorants, it is an expert diver and hunts fish by swimming underwater. Because their feathers are not fully waterproof, they are often seen standing on rocks or branches with their wings spread out to dry. They are social birds and often nest in colonies.

The Great Egret (Ardea alba), also known as the common egret, large egret, or (in the Old World) great white egret or great white heron, is a large, widely distributed wading bird, with four subspecies found in Asia, Africa, the Americas, and southern Europe. Distributed across most of the tropical and warmer temperate regions of the world, it builds tree nests in colonies close to water. The Great Egret is a large heron with all-white plumage. It can reach 1 m (3.3 ft) in height and weigh up to 950 g (2.1 lb). It is only slightly smaller than the great blue or grey heron. It can be distinguished from other white egrets by its yellow bill and black legs and feet. It feeds in shallow water on fish, frogs, and insects.

The Green Heron (Butorides virescens) is a small heron of North and Central America. It was long considered conspecific with the striated heron (Butorides striata), and together they were called the 'green-backed heron'. Green Herons are relatively small birds, with a length of 41–48 cm (16–19 in). They have a dark-green cap, a chestnut neck, and grey-blue wings. They are found in a variety of wetland habitats, including swamps, marshes, and the edges of ponds and rivers. One of the most remarkable behaviors of the Green Heron is its use of bait. It is one of the few bird species known to use tools; it will drop small objects like feathers, insects, or twigs on the water's surface to attract fish. They are solitary and secretive birds.

The Turkey Vulture (Cathartes aura), also known in some North American regions as the turkey buzzard (or just buzzard), and in some areas of the Caribbean as the John crow or carrion crow, is the most widespread of the New World vultures. One of three species in the genus Cathartes of the family Cathartidae, the turkey vulture ranges from southern Canada to the southernmost tip of South America. It inhabits a variety of open and semi-open areas, including subtropical forests, shrublands, pastures, and deserts. Like all New World vultures, it is not closely related to the Old World vultures of Europe, Africa, and Asia. It is a scavenger and feeds almost exclusively on carrion. It finds its food using its keen eyesight and sense of smell, flying low enough to detect the gases produced by the beginnings of the process of decay in dead animals.

The Cooper's Hawk (Accipiter cooperii) is a medium-sized hawk native to the North American continent and found from southern Canada to northern Mexico. As in many birds of prey, the male is smaller than the female. The birds found east of the Mississippi River tend to be larger than the birds found to the west. Cooper's Hawks are specialized for hunting small to medium-sized birds, which they catch in mid-air or pluck from the canopy using their incredible agility in flight through forested areas. They have a blue-grey back and a reddish-barred breast. Their tail is long and rounded with dark bands and a white tip. They are often seen in woodlands, but have also adapted well to suburban areas where they hunt at bird feeders.

The Red-shouldered Hawk (Buteo lineatus) is a medium-sized hawk. Its breeding range spans eastern North America and along the coast of California and northern-to-central-western Mexico. It is a permanent resident throughout most of its range, though northern birds do migrate, mostly to central Mexico. The Red-shouldered Hawk is a bird of forested wetlands and riparian areas. It has a brownish head, a reddish-brown barred breast, and a dark back with white spotting. The namesake 'red shoulders' are actually the bird's lesser covert feathers, which are a vibrant rufous color. These hawks are very vocal, especially during the breeding season, and have a distinctive, repetitive 'kee-rah' call. They hunt small mammals, reptiles, and amphibians.

The Common Moorhen (Gallinula chloropus), also known as the swamp chicken or waterhen, is a bird species in the rail family (Rallidae). It is distributed across much of the Old World. The Common Moorhen lives around well-vegetated marshes, ponds, canals and other wetlands. The species is not found in the polar regions or many tropical rainforests. Elsewhere it is likely the most common rail species, except for the Eurasian coot in some regions. The bird is dark grey and black with a brown back and a white stripe on the flank. It has a distinctive red shield on its forehead and a yellow-tipped red bill. Its legs are greenish-yellow with a red band on the tibia. They are omnivorous and feed on a variety of plant and animal material.

The Sora (Porzana carolina) is a small waterbird of the rail family, Rallidae. The name 'Sora' is likely an onomatopoeic imitation of the bird's call. It is the most common and widely distributed rail in North America, breeding in marshes and wet meadows across much of Canada and the northern United States. Soras are migratory, wintering in the southern United States, the Caribbean, and Central America. They are small, secretive birds, about 20–25 cm (7.9–9.8 in) long, with a short, yellow bill and a black face and throat. The body is greyish-brown with white spotting. They are more often heard than seen, with a variety of calls including a loud, descending whinny. They feed on seeds and small invertebrates in the mud.

The Purple Gallinule (Porphyrio martinica) is a large, brightly colored swamp bird in the rail family, Rallidae. It is found in the southeastern United States, Central America, the Caribbean, and South America. This bird is renowned for its spectacular plumage: the adult has a purple-blue head and underparts, a green back, and a light blue frontal shield on its forehead. Its bill is red with a yellow tip, and it has extremely long, yellow legs and toes which allow it to walk easily across floating vegetation like lily pads. Purple Gallinules are found in freshwater marshes with abundant floating plants. They are omnivorous, eating a variety of seeds, fruit, and small animals including insects and frogs.

The Sandhill Crane (Antigone canadensis) is a species of large crane of North America and extreme northeastern Siberia. The common name of this bird refers to habitat like that at the Platte River, on the edge of Nebraska's Sandhills in the American Midwest. This is the most numerous crane species in the world. Sandhill Cranes are large, grey birds with a red crown on their forehead. They are known for their spectacular courtship dances, which involve leaping into the air, bowing, and stretching their wings. They have a loud, trumpeting call that can be heard from a great distance. Sandhill Cranes are migratory and are often seen in large flocks during their journey. They inhabit wetlands and open grasslands.

The Sanderling (Calidris alba) is a small, prolific shorebird that has a circumpolar breeding distribution in the Arctic. It is a highly migratory bird, wintering on coastal beaches throughout the world. It is recognizable by its characteristic behavior of running in and out with the waves on sandy beaches, searching for small invertebrates. The sanderling is a small sandpiper, 18–20 cm (7.1–7.9 in) in length. In the winter, it is one of the palest shorebirds, with a light grey back and a white underside. In its breeding plumage, the head and neck are a reddish-brown color. Unlike most other sandpipers, the sanderling lacks a hind toe, which helps it run quickly on the sand. They are often seen in small flocks on the shoreline.

The Laughing Gull (Leucophaeus atricilla) is a medium-sized gull of North and South America. It breeds on the Atlantic coast of North America, the Caribbean, and northern South America. Northern populations migrate south in winter. The name 'Laughing Gull' comes from its loud and raucous 'ha-ha-ha-ha-ha' call, which sounds like human laughter. The adult in breeding plumage has a black hood, a white body, and dark grey wings with black tips. The bill and legs are dark red. In non-breeding plumage, the black hood is lost and replaced by a greyish wash on the head. They are commonly found along coastal areas, beaches, and salt marshes, where they scavenge for food and catch small fish and insects.

The Common Tern (Sterna hirundo) is a seabird in the family Laridae. This bird has a circumpolar distribution, its four subspecies breeding in temperate and subarctic regions of Europe, Asia and North America. It is strongly migratory, wintering in coastal tropical and subtropical oceans. Breeding adults have light grey upperparts, white to very light grey underparts, a black cap, orange-red legs, and a narrow pointed bill. Depending on the subspecies, the bill can be mostly red with a black tip, or all black. Common Terns are graceful fliers and are known for their dramatic plunge-dives into the water to catch small fish. They nest in colonies on islands, beaches, and sometimes on floating vegetation in inland lakes.

The Mourning Dove (Zenaida macroura) is a member of the dove family, Columbidae. The bird is also known as the American mourning dove, the rain dove, and colloquially as the turtle dove, and was once known as the Carolina pigeon and Carolina turtledove. It is one of the most abundant and widespread of all North American birds. Its ability to sustain its numbers despite being one of the most popular game birds in North America is due to its high reproductive rate; in warm areas, a pair may raise up to six broods a year. The name 'Mourning Dove' comes from its distinctive, mournful-sounding 'coo-OO-oo-oo' call. They are primarily seed-eaters and are often seen at bird feeders and on the ground.

The Belted Kingfisher (Megaceryle alcyon) is a large, conspicuous water kingfisher, native to North America. It is the only member of the kingfisher family found in most of its range. This bird is a frequent sight along the edges of ponds, lakes, and rivers, where it perches on branches or hovers over the water to search for small fish. It has a large head with a shaggy crest and a long, stout, black bill. The plumage is primarily blue-grey with a white collar and underparts. Females have a reddish-brown band across the belly, which is absent in males. Its call is a loud, harsh rattle. They nest in burrows which they dig into the soil of vertical river banks.

The Red-bellied Woodpecker (Melanerpes carolinus) is a medium-sized woodpecker of the family Picidae. It breeds mainly in the eastern United States, ranging as far south as Florida and as far north as Canada. Its common name is somewhat misleading, as the red on its belly is often subtle and difficult to see in the field; a more prominent feature is the vibrant red cap and nape on the male, and the red nape on the female. The back and wings are marked with a black and white 'zebra' pattern. These woodpeckers are highly adaptable and are found in a variety of forest types as well as in residential areas. They eat a variety of insects, fruits, and nuts, and are known for caching food for later use.

The Downy Woodpecker (Dryobates pubescens) is a species of woodpecker, the smallest in North America. It is found across most of the continent, from the treeline in Alaska and Canada to the Gulf Coast and the southwestern United States. It is a very common and familiar bird, often seen in backyards and at bird feeders. It is black and white with a white back and a white belly. Males have a small red patch on the back of their head, which is absent in females. The Downy Woodpecker is very similar in appearance to the larger Hairy Woodpecker, but can be distinguished by its smaller size and shorter bill. They feed primarily on insects, especially those found in the crevices of tree bark.

The Eastern Phoebe (Sayornis phoebe) is a small passerine bird, a member of the tyrant flycatcher family. It is found in eastern North America, breeding from northern Canada to the southern United States. It is a migratory bird, wintering in the southern United States and Mexico. The Eastern Phoebe is a plain-looking bird, with grey-brown upperparts and off-white underparts. It is easily identified by its habit of constantly wagging its tail and its repetitive 'fee-bee' call. It is one of the first birds to return to its breeding grounds in the spring. They often nest on human-made structures such as bridges and buildings. They feed primarily on insects, which they catch in flight.

The Loggerhead Shrike (Lanius ludovicianus) is a passerine bird belonging to the shrike family, Laniidae. It is the only member of the shrike family endemic to North America. The name 'loggerhead' refers to the bird's relatively large head. This bird is famous for its predatory behavior, often referred to as the 'butcherbird' because it impales its prey—insects, small reptiles, and even small birds and mammals—on thorns, barbed wire, or sharp twigs. This behavior helps the bird secure its food and overcome its lack of powerful talons. The Loggerhead Shrike is black, white, and grey with a distinctive black mask on its face. It is found in open country with scattered trees and shrubs.

The Warbling Vireo (Vireo gilvus) is a small North American songbird. Its breeding habitat is open deciduous forests from Alaska and across southern Canada to the northern United States. It is a migratory bird, wintering in Central America and the Caribbean. The Warbling Vireo is a plain-looking bird, with grey-olive upperparts and white underparts with a yellowish wash on the flanks. It lacks prominent markings like wing bars or eye rings, but has a pale eyebrow. It is best known for its long, complex, and melodious song, which it often sings from high in the canopy. It feeds primarily on insects, especially caterpillars, and also eats some berries.

The American Crow (Corvus brachyrhynchos) is a large passerine bird species of the family Corvidae. It is a common bird found throughout much of North America. American crows are all black, with iridescent feathers that can show hints of blue or purple in the sunlight. They are highly intelligent and social birds, often seen in large groups called murders. They are well-known for their problem-solving abilities and their complex social structures. American crows are omnivorous and will eat almost anything, including insects, grain, fruit, small animals, and human food scraps. They are also known for their loud and familiar 'caw-caw' call. They have adapted exceptionally well to human-dominated landscapes.

The Tufted Duck (Aythya fuligula) is a medium-sized diving duck with a population of close to one million birds. It is found in Eurasia and North Africa, where it breeds in a variety of freshwater habitats including lakes, ponds, and slow-moving rivers. It is a migratory bird, wintering in warmer regions. The adult male is all black except for white flanks and a blue-grey bill; it has a long, thin crest (tuft) on the back of its head. The female is brown with paler flanks and a shorter tuft. Tufted Ducks feed primarily by diving for aquatic invertebrates, especially mollusks and insects, as well as some aquatic plants. They are social birds and often seen in large groups on open water.

The European Starling (Sturnus vulgaris), also known as the Common Starling, is a medium-sized passerine bird in the starling family, Sturnidae. It is native to most of temperate Europe and western Asia, but has been introduced to many other parts of the world, including North America, Australasia, and South Africa. The European Starling is a highly adaptable and successful species, often considered a pest in areas where it is not native due to its tendency to form massive flocks (murmurations) that can cause damage to crops and compete with native bird species. It is black with a metallic sheen and is covered in white spots at certain times of the year. They are excellent mimics and have a wide variety of complex calls.

The Tree Swallow (Tachycineta bicolor) is a migratory passerine bird in the swallow family. It is found in North America, breeding from Alaska across Canada to the northern and central United States. It is an early migrant, returning to its breeding grounds as soon as the weather permits. Tree Swallows are strikingly beautiful, with iridescent blue-green upperparts and stark white underparts. They are highly aerial and catch insects in flight, often skimming over the surface of the water. As their name suggests, they are cavity-nesters and traditionally used holes in trees made by woodpeckers, but they have also adapted exceptionally well to nest boxes. They are often seen in large flocks near wetlands and open fields.

The Bushtit (Psaltriparus minimus) is a very small passerine bird in the family Aegithalidae. It is the only member of its family found in the New World. It is found in western North America, from southern British Columbia through the western United States to central Mexico. The Bushtit is a tiny, plain-looking bird, greyish-brown overall with a long tail and a short bill. They are highly social and are almost always seen in small, active flocks that move through shrubs and trees in search of insects. One of their most remarkable features is their nest, which is a long, hanging, sock-like structure made of moss, lichen, and spider webs. They are very active and constantly chatter to each other while foraging.

The Northern Mockingbird (Mimus polyglottos) is a bird in the family Mimidae, native to North America. This bird is best known for its incredible ability to mimic the songs of dozens of other bird species, as well as insects, amphibians, and even mechanical sounds. A single male mockingbird can have a repertoire of over 200 different songs. It is a medium-sized songbird, grey-brown above and pale below, with large white patches on its wings and tail that are very visible in flight. Mockingbirds are highly territorial and will aggressively defend their nests against much larger animals, including cats and dogs. They are common in a variety of habitats, including residential areas, where they are often seen on lawns.

Oak is a tree or shrub in the genus Quercus of the beech family, Fagaceae. There are approximately 500 extant species of oaks. The name 'oak' can also appear in the names of species in related genera, notably Lithocarpus (stone oaks), as well as in those of unrelated species such as Grevillea robusta (silky oaks) and the Casuarinaceae (she-oaks). The genus Quercus is native to the Northern Hemisphere, and includes deciduous and evergreen species extending from cool temperate to tropical latitudes in the Americas, Asia, Europe, and North Africa. Oaks are known for their strong, durable wood, which has been used for centuries in construction and furniture making, and for their acorns, which are an important food source for many wildlife species.

Sequoia sempervirens is the sole living species of the genus Sequoia in the cypress family Cupressaceae. Common names include coast redwood, coastal redwood, and California redwood. It is an evergreen, long-lived, monoecious tree living 1,200–2,200 years or more. This species includes the tallest living trees on Earth, reaching up to 115.5 m (379.1 ft) in height and up to 8.9 m (29.2 ft) in diameter at breast height. These trees are native to coastal regions of northern California and southwestern Oregon in the United States. Coast redwoods are known for their massive size, reddish-brown bark, and their ability to thrive in the cool, moist climate of the Pacific coast fog belt.

Lavandula (common name lavender) is a genus of 47 known species of flowering plants in the mint family, Lamiaceae. It is native to the Old World and is found from Cape Verde and the Canary Islands, Europe across to northern and eastern Africa, the Mediterranean, southwest Asia to southeast India. Many members of the genus are cultivated extensively in temperate climates as ornamental plants for garden and landscape use, for use as culinary herbs, and also commercially for the extraction of essential oils. The most widely cultivated species, Lavandula angustifolia, is often referred to as lavender, and there is a color named for it. Lavender is known for its beautiful purple flowers and its soothing, aromatic fragrance.

Bamboos are a diverse group of evergreen perennial flowering plants in the subfamily Bambusoideae of the grass family Poaceae. The word 'bamboo' comes from the Dutch or Portuguese languages, which probably borrowed it from Malay. In bamboo, as in other grasses, the internodal regions of the stem are usually hollow and the vascular bundles in the cross section are scattered throughout the stem instead of in a cylindrical arrangement. Bamboos include some of the fastest-growing plants in the world, due to a unique rhizome-dependent system. Certain species of bamboo can grow 910 mm (36 in) within a 24-hour period, at a rate of almost 40 mm (1.6 in) an hour. Bamboos are of notable economic and cultural significance in South Asia, Southeast Asia, and East Asia.

A cactus (plural cacti, cactuses, or less commonly cactus) is a member of the plant family Cactaceae, a family comprising about 127 genera with some 1750 known species of the order Caryophyllales. The word 'cactus' derives, through Latin, from the Ancient Greek word kaktos, a name originally used for a spiny plant whose identity is not certain. Cacti occur in a wide range of shapes and sizes. Most cacti live in habitats subject to at least some drought. Many live in extremely dry environments, even being found in the Atacama Desert, one of the driest places on Earth. Cacti show many adaptations to conserve water. Almost all cacti are succulents, meaning they have thickened, fleshy parts adapted to store water.

The Venus flytrap (Dionaea muscipula) is a carnivorous plant native to subtropical wetlands on the East Coast of the United States in North Carolina and South Carolina. It catches its prey—chiefly insects and arachnids—with a trapping structure formed by the terminal portion of each of the plant's leaves, which is triggered by tiny hairs on their inner surfaces. When an insect or spider crawling along the leaves contacts a hair, the trap prepares to close, snapping shut only if another hair is touched within approximately twenty seconds of the first strike. The speed of closing can vary depending on humidity, light, size of prey, and general growing conditions. The Venus flytrap is one of a very small group of plants capable of rapid movement.

Maple is a common name for the genus Acer, which contains about 128 species of trees and shrubs in the soapberry family Sapindaceae. Most species are native to Asia, with a number also appearing in Europe, northern Africa, and North America. Only one species, Acer laurinum, extends to the Southern Hemisphere. The type species of the genus is the sycamore maple, Acer pseudoplatanus, the most common maple species in Europe. Maples are most easily recognized by their distinctive opposite leaf arrangement and their winged fruits, known as samaras or 'helicopter' seeds. They are also famous for their vibrant autumn foliage and for Being the source of maple syrup, which is produced from the sap of species such as the sugar maple.

Adansonia is a genus of deciduous trees known as baobabs. They are found in arid regions of Madagascar, mainland Africa, and Australia. The generic name honors Michel Adanson, the French naturalist and explorer who described Adansonia digitata. Baobabs are long-lived and are often referred to as 'upside-down trees' because their sparse branches look like roots. They are famous for their massive, water-storing trunks, which can reach enormous diameters. This allows them to survive in harsh, dry environments. The fruit of the baobab is edible and is rich in vitamin C. In many cultures, baobabs are considered sacred and are often the center of community life. They provide shelter, food, and water for both humans and wildlife.

Nymphaeaceae is a family of flowering plants, commonly called water lilies. They live as rhizomatous aquatic herbs in temperate and tropical climates around the world. The family contains five genera with about 70 species. Water lilies are rooted in soil in bodies of water, with leaves and flowers floating on or emergent from the surface. The leaves are round, with a radial notch in Nymphaea and Nuphar, but fully circular in Victoria and Euryale. Water lilies are not only beautiful but also play an important role in aquatic ecosystems by providing shade and shelter for fish and other aquatic life. They have been featured in art and literature for centuries and are often associated with peace and tranquility.

Ginkgo biloba, commonly known as ginkgo or gingko, also known as the maidenhair tree, is a species of tree native to China. It is the only extant species in the order Ginkgoales, which first appeared over 290 million years ago. Fossils very similar to the living species, belonging to the genus Ginkgo, extend back to the Middle Jurassic approximately 170 million years ago. The tree is widely cultivated, and was introduced early to human history. It has various uses as a traditional medicine and as a source of food. The ginkgo has a distinctive fan-shaped leaf and is known for its remarkable resilience and longevity. It is often referred to as a 'living fossil' because it has remained virtually unchanged for millions of years.

Amorphophallus titanum, the titan arum, is a flowering plant in the family Araceae. It has the largest unbranched inflorescence in the world. The inflorescence of the talipot palm, Corypha umbraculifera, is larger, but it is branched rather than unbranched. A. titanum is endemic to Sumatra. Due to its odor, which is like the smell of a rotting animal, the titan arum is characterized as a carrion flower, and is also known as the corpse flower or corpse plant. The same name is also used for the genus Rafflesia which, like the titan arum, grows in the rainforests of Sumatra. The massive flower can reach over 3 meters in height. It blooms infrequently, and the bloom only lasts for a few days, attracting pollinating carrion beetles and flesh flies.

Pitcher plants are several different carnivorous plants which have evolved modified leaves known as pitfall traps—a prey-trapping mechanism featuring a deep cavity filled with digestive liquid. The traps of what are considered to be 'true' pitcher plants are formed by specialized leaves. These plants attract insects with nectar and colorful markings. Once an insect lands on the slippery rim of the pitcher, it slides down into the liquid at the bottom, where it is drowned and digested by enzymes. This allows the plant to obtain nutrients, such as nitrogen and phosphorus, that are often lacking in the poor soils where they grow. There are several different families of pitcher plants, including the Nepenthaceae (tropical pitcher plants) and the Sarraceniaceae (North American pitcher plants).

Macrocystis pyrifera, commonly known as giant kelp, is a species of kelp (large brown alga), and one of four species in the genus Macrocystis. Giant kelp is common along the coast of the eastern Pacific Ocean, from southwest Alaska to Mexico, and in the Southern Ocean near South Africa, New Zealand, and Australia. Individual algae may grow to more than 45 m (150 ft) long at a rate of as much as 60 cm (2 ft) per day. Giant kelp grows in dense 'forests' that provide a vital habitat for a huge variety of marine life, including fish, sea urchins, and sea otters. It is anchored to the seafloor by a structure called a holdfast and uses gas-filled bladders called pneumatocysts to keep its fronds floating near the surface for photosynthesis.

Drosera, commonly known as the sundews, is one of the largest genera of carnivorous plants, with at least 194 species. These members of the family Droseraceae lure, capture, and digest insects using stalked mucilaginous glands covering their leaf surfaces. The insects are used to supplement the poor mineral nutrition of the soil in which the plants grow. Various species, which vary greatly in size and form, are native to every continent except Antarctica. The name 'sundew' comes from the glistening droplets of mucilage on the glands that resemble morning dew. When an insect becomes stuck in the 'dew', the leaf slowly curls around it to increase the surface area for digestion. Sundews are found in damp, nutrient-poor habitats such as bogs and fens.

Leontopodium nivale, commonly called edelweiss, is a mountain flower belonging to the daisy or sunflower family Asteraceae. The plant prefers rocky limestone places at about 1,800–3,000 metres (5,900–9,800 ft) altitude. It is non-toxic and has been used in traditional medicine as a remedy against abdominal and respiratory diseases. The leaves and flowers are covered with dense hairs, which appear to protect the plant from cold, aridity, and ultraviolet radiation. It is a scarce, short-lived flower found in remote mountain areas and has been used as a symbol for alpinism, for rugged beauty and purity associated with the Alps and Carpathians, and as a national symbol, especially of Austria, Bulgaria, Romania, Slovenia, and Switzerland.

Rafflesia is a genus of parasitic flowering plants in the family Euphorbiaceae. The species have very large flowers, the buds rising from the ground or directly from the lower stems of their host plants; one species has the largest flowers in the world. The genus is native to Southeast Asia, in Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, and the Philippines. Rafflesia is a parasite on several species of Tetrastigma (vines in the grape family). It has no visible leaves, stems, or roots; it is entirely dependent on its host plant for water and nutrients. The flower is famous for its massive size and its smell of rotting meat, which attracts pollinating flies. The most famous species, Rafflesia arnoldii, can produce a flower over 100 cm (39 in) in diameter.

Sequoiadendron giganteum (giant sequoia; also known as giant redwood, Sierra redwood, Sierran redwood, Wellingtonia or simply big tree) is the sole living species in the genus Sequoiadendron, and one of three species of coniferous trees known as redwoods. Giant sequoia specimens are the most massive individual trees in the world and the largest living organisms on Earth by volume. They grow to an average height of 50–85 m (164–279 ft) with trunk diameters ranging from 6–8 m (20–26 ft). Record specimens have been measured to be 94.8 m (311 ft) tall. These trees are native to the western slopes of the Sierra Nevada mountains in California. They are known for their massive, reddish-brown bark and their incredible longevity, with some specimens estimated to be over 3,000 years old.

Yucca brevifolia is a plant-like species belonging to the genus Yucca. It is tree-like in habit, which is reflected in its common names Joshua tree, yucca palm, tree yucca, and palm tree yucca. This monocotyledonous tree is native to the arid Southwestern United States, specifically the Mojave Desert at elevations between 400 and 1,800 m (1,300 and 5,900 ft). It is known for its distinctive, contorted branches and spiky leaves. The Joshua tree is a vital part of the desert ecosystem, providing habitat and food for a variety of wildlife. It relies on a unique symbiotic relationship with the yucca moth for pollination. These trees are slow-growing and can live for hundreds of years, but they are increasingly threatened by climate change and habitat destruction.

The Orchidaceae are a diverse and widespread family of flowering plants, with blooms that are often colourful and fragrant, commonly known as the orchid family. Along with the Asteraceae, they are one of the two largest families of flowering plants. The Orchidaceae have about 28,000 currently accepted species, distributed in about 763 genera. The determination of which family is larger is still under debate, because verified data on the members of such enormous families are continually in flux. Regardless, the number of orchid species is nearly equal to the number of bony fishes, more than twice the number of bird species, and about four times the number of mammal species. Orchids are known for their complex and highly specialized flowers, many of which have evolved unique relationships with specific pollinators.

Coffea is a genus of flowering plants whose seeds, called coffee beans, are used to make various coffee beverages and products. It is a member of the family Rubiaceae. They are shrubs or small trees native to tropical and southern Africa and tropical Asia. Coffee ranks as one of the world's most valuable and widely traded commodity crops and is an important export product of several countries, including those in Central and South America, the Caribbean and Africa. The two most economically important species are Coffea arabica (Arabica coffee) and Coffea canephora (Robusta coffee). Coffee plants are evergreens with glossy leaves and fragrant white flowers that produce red or purple berries. The caffeine in the seeds acts as a natural pesticide, protecting the plant from insects.

Theobroma cacao, also called the cacao tree and the cocoa tree, is a small (4–8 m (13–26 ft) tall) evergreen tree in the family Malvaceae, native to the deep tropical regions of the Americas. Its seeds, cocoa beans, are used to make chocolate liquor, cocoa solids, cocoa butter and chocolate. The name of the genus, Theobroma, means 'food of the gods' in Greek. Cocoa trees are native to the Amazon rainforest and thrive in the hot, humid conditions of the tropical understory. The tree produces large, colorful pods that contain between 20 and 60 seeds (beans) embedded in a sweet, white pulp. Cacao has been cultivated for thousands of years, originally by the Olmec, Maya, and Aztec civilizations for use in ritual beverages and as a form of currency.

Cotton is a soft, fluffy staple fiber that grows in a boll, or protective case, around the seeds of the cotton plants of the genus Gossypium in the mallow family Malvaceae. The fiber is almost pure cellulose. Under natural conditions, the cotton bolls will increase the dispersal of the seeds. The plant is a shrub native to tropical and subtropical regions around the world, including the Americas, Africa, Egypt and India. The greatest diversity of wild cotton species is found in Mexico, followed by Australia and Africa. Cotton was independently domesticated in the Old and New Worlds. The fiber is most often spun into yarn or thread and used to make a soft, breathable textile. Cotton is one of the most important non-food crops in the world and has a significant impact on global trade and history.

Camellia sinensis is a species of evergreen shrubs or small trees in the flowering plant family Theaceae whose leaves and leaf buds are used to produce tea. Common names include 'tea plant', 'tea shrub', and 'tea tree' (not to be confused with Melaleuca alternifolia, the source of tea tree oil). White tea, yellow tea, green tea, oolong, dark tea (which includes pu-erh tea) and black tea are all harvested from one of two major varieties grown today, Camellia sinensis var. sinensis and C. s. var. assamica, but are processed differently to attain varying levels of oxidation. After harvesting, tea leaves are withered, rolled, and dried to produce various types of tea. Tea is the second most-consumed beverage in the world, after water, and has deep cultural and economic significance.

The olive, known by the botanical name Olea europaea, meaning 'European olive', is a species of small tree in the family Oleaceae, found in the Mediterranean Basin from Portugal to the Levant, the Arabian Peninsula, and southern Asia as far east as China, as well as the Canary Islands and Réunion. The species is cultivated in many places and considered naturalized in all the countries of the Mediterranean coast, as well as in Argentina, Saudi Arabia, Java, Norfolk Island, California, and Bermuda. The olive's fruit, also called an olive, is of major agricultural importance in the Mediterranean region as the source of olive oil. The tree and its fruit give their name to the plant family, which also includes species such as lilacs, jasmine, Forsythia, and true ash trees. Olive trees are famous for their longevity and are a symbol of peace.

The apple tree (Malus domestica) is a deciduous tree in the rose family best known for its sweet, pomaceous fruit, the apple. It is cultivated worldwide as a fruit tree, and is the most widely grown species in the genus Malus. The tree originated in Central Asia, where its wild ancestor, Malus sieversii, is still found today. Apples have been grown for thousands of years in Asia and Europe and were brought to North America by European colonists. Apples have religious and mythological significance in many cultures, including Norse, Greek, and European Christian tradition. Apple trees are large if grown from seed, but generally smaller if grafted onto roots (rootstocks). There are more than 7,500 known cultivars of apples, resulting in a range of desired characteristics and tastes.

The potato is a starchy, tuberous crop from the perennial nightshade Solanum tuberosum. Potato may be used to refer to the plant itself as well as the edible tuber. In the region of the Andes, there are some other closely related cultivated potato species. Potatoes were introduced to Europe from the Americas in the second half of the 16th century by the Spanish. Today they are a staple food in many parts of the world and an integral part of much of the world's food supply. It is the world's fourth-largest food crop, after maize, wheat and rice. The wild potato species, originating in modern-day southern Peru and extreme northwestern Bolivia, were domesticated between 7,000 and 10,000 years ago. Following centuries of selective breeding, there are now over 5,000 different types of potatoes.

The tomato is the edible berry of the plant Solanum lycopersicum, commonly known as a tomato plant. The species originated in western South America and Central America. The Nahuatl word tomatl gave rise to the Spanish word tomate, from which the English word tomato derived. Its use as a cultivated food originated with the indigenous peoples of Mexico. The Spanish encountered the tomato from their contact with the Aztecs during the Spanish colonization of the Americas, and brought it to Europe. From there, the tomato was introduced to other parts of the European-colonized world during the 16th century. Tomatoes are a significant source of umami flavor. The tomato is consumed in diverse ways, raw or cooked, in many dishes, sauces, salads, and drinks.

Maize, also known as corn, is a cereal grain first domesticated by indigenous peoples in southern Mexico about 10,000 years ago. The leafy stalk of the plant produces polleniferous inflorescences and separate ovuliferous inflorescences called ears that yield kernels or seeds, which are fruits. Maize has become a staple food in many parts of the world, with total production of maize surpassing that of wheat or rice. In addition to being consumed directly by humans (often in the form of masa), maize is also used for corn ethanol, animal feed and other maize products, such as corn starch and corn syrup. The six major types of maize are dent corn, flint corn, pod corn, popcorn, flour corn, and sweet corn. Sugar-rich varieties called sweet corn are usually grown for human consumption as kernels, while field corn varieties are used for animal feed.

Sugarcane or sugar cane refers to several species and hybrids of tall perennial grasses in the genus Saccharum, tribe Andropogoneae, used for sugar production. The plants are 2 to 6 m (6 ft 7 in to 19 ft 8 in) tall with stout, jointed, fibrous stalks that are rich in sucrose, which accumulates in the stalk internodes. Sugarcane belongs to the grass family, Poaceae, an economically important flowering plant family that includes maize, wheat, rice, and sorghum, and many forage crops. It is native to the warm temperate to tropical regions of Southeast Asia and New Guinea. In addition to sugar, products derived from sugarcane include falernum, molasses, rum, cachaça, and bagasse. Sugarcane is the world's largest crop by production quantity, with 1.9 billion tonnes produced in 2020.

The soybean or soya bean (Glycine max) is a species of legume native to East Asia, widely grown for its edible bean, which has numerous uses. Traditional unfermented food uses of soybeans include soy milk, from which tofu and tofu skin are made. Fermented soy foods include soy sauce, fermented bean paste, nattō, and tempeh. Fat-free soybean meal is a significant and cheap source of protein for animal feeds and many packaged meals. For example, soybean products, such as textured vegetable protein (TVP), are ingredients in many meat and dairy substitutes. The beans contain significant amounts of phytic acid, dietary minerals and B vitamins. Soya vegetable oil, used in food and industrial applications, is another product of processing the soybean crop.

The peanut, also known as the groundnut, goober, pindar or monkey nut, and taxonomically classified as Arachis hypogaea, is a legume crop grown mainly for its edible seeds. It is widely grown in the tropics and subtropics, being important to both small and large commercial producers. It is classified as both a grain legume and, due to its high oil content, an oil crop. Atypical among legume crop plants, peanut pods develop underground (geocarpy) rather than above ground. This characteristic was known to the Linnaeus, who gave the species the name hypogaea, which means 'under the earth'. Peanuts are native to South America and were introduced to other parts of the world by Spanish and Portuguese explorers. They are a significant source of protein and oil and are consumed in many forms, including roasted, as peanut butter, and in oil.

A grape is a fruit, botanically a berry, of the deciduous woody vines of the flowering plant genus Vitis. Grapes can be eaten fresh as table grapes, used for making wine, jam, grape juice, jelly, grape seed extract, raisins, vinegar, and grape seed oil. Grapes are a non-climacteric type of fruit, generally occurring in clusters. The cultivation of the domesticated grape began 6,000–8,000 years ago in the Near East. The earliest archaeological evidence for a dominant position of wine-making in human culture dates from 8,000 years ago in Georgia. Grapes come in many colors, including green, red, black, and purple. They are a good source of vitamins C and K and antioxidants like resveratrol, which is found in the skins of red and purple grapes.

A pine is any conifer in the genus Pinus of the family Pinaceae. Pinus is the sole genus in the subfamily Pinoideae. The Plant List compiled by the Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew and Missouri Botanical Garden accepts 126 species names of pines as current, together with 35 unresolved species and many more synonyms. Pines are evergreen, coniferous resinous trees (or, rarely, shrubs) growing 3–80 m (10–260 ft) tall, with the majority of species reaching 15–45 m (50–150 ft) tall. The smallest are Siberian dwarf pine and Potosi pinyon, and the tallest is an 81.79 m (268.35 ft) tall ponderosa pine located in southern Oregon's Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest. Pines are long-lived and are found across the Northern Hemisphere. Their wood and resin are important industrial products.

Firs (Abies) are a genus of 48–56 species of evergreen coniferous trees in the family Pinaceae. They are found through much of North and Central America, Europe, Asia, and North Africa, occurring in mountains over most of the range. Firs are most closely related to the genus Cedrus (cedar). Douglas firs are not true firs, being of the genus Pseudotsuga. Firs are large trees, reaching heights of 10–80 m (33–262 ft) and trunk diameters of 0.5–4 m (1 ft 8 in – 13 ft 1 in) when mature. Firs can be distinguished from other members of the pine family by the unique attachment of their needle-like leaves and by their erect, cylindrical cones. Many fir species are used as Christmas trees due to their symmetrical shape and ability to hold their needles for a long time after being cut.

Cypress is a common name for various coniferous trees or shrubs of northern temperate regions that belong to the family Cupressaceae. The word 'cypress' is derived from Old French cipres, which was borrowed from Latin cypressus, the latinisation of the Greek kyparissos. The most famous species is the Mediterranean cypress (Cupressus sempervirens), known for its tall, flame-like shape and its association with cemeteries and mourning in Mediterranean cultures. However, the term 'cypress' is also used for many other genera in the family Cupressaceae, such as Taxodium (bald cypress) and Chamaecyparis (false cypress). Cypress trees are known for their durable, rot-resistant wood and many species are used in landscaping and for timber.

A birch is a thin-leaved deciduous hardwood tree of the genus Betula, in the family Betulaceae, which also includes alders, hazels, and hornbeams. It is closely related to the beech-oak family Fagaceae. The genus Betula contains 30 to 60 known taxa of which 11 are on the IUCN 2011 Red List of Threatened Species. They are generally small to medium-sized trees or shrubs, mostly of northern temperate and boreal climates. Birch species are generally short-lived, pioneer species widespread in the Northern Hemisphere, particularly in northern regions of temperate climates and in boreal climates. They have a characteristic smooth, papery bark that often peels in thin layers. Birch wood is light-colored and fine-grained, used for furniture, flooring, and plywood.

Aspen is a common name for several tree species; some are classified by some biologists in the section Populus, of the Populus genus. Aspens are native to cold regions with cool summers, in the north of the Northern Hemisphere, extending south at high-altitude areas such as mountains or high plains. They are all medium-sized deciduous trees reaching 15–30 m (49–98 ft) tall. Aspens are known for the way their leaves 'quake' or 'tremble' in even a light breeze, which is caused by the flattened shape of their leaf stalks. This gives rise to the name 'quaking aspen' (Populus tremuloides). Aspens are also remarkable for their ability to reproduce through root sprouts, often forming large clonal colonies that are essentially a single organism. One such colony in Utah, named Pando, is estimated to be over 80,000 years old.

Willows, also called sallows and osiers, from the genus Salix, around 400 species of deciduous trees and shrubs, found primarily on moist soils in cold and temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere. Most species are known as willow, but some narrow-leaved shrub species are called osier, and some broader-leaved species are referred to as sallow. Willows all have abundant watery bark sap, which is heavily charged with salicylic acid, soft, usually tough, pliant wood, slender branches, and large, fibrous, often stoloniferous roots. The roots are remarkable for their toughness, size, and tenacity of life, and the trees will sprout readily from any part of the root. Willows are often used for bank stabilization and as ornamental trees near water.

A cherry blossom, also known as a Japanese cherry or sakura, is a flower of many trees of genus Prunus or Prunus subg. Cerasus. They are native to the Northern Hemisphere, particularly East Asia, including Japan, Korea and China. They generally refer to ornamental cherry trees, not cherry trees that produce fruit for eating. It is considered the national flower of Japan. Cherry blossoms are a symbol of renewal and the fleeting nature of life, and their blooming is celebrated with festivals called hanami. The flowers range from white to dark pink and typically bloom in early spring. They are widely planted in parks and gardens for their spectacular and delicate beauty.

Hydrangea (common names hydrangea or hortensia) is a genus of over 75 species of flowering plants native to Asia and the Americas. By far the greatest species diversity is in eastern Asia, notably China, Korea, and Japan. Most are shrubs 1 to 3 meters tall, but some are small trees, and others lianas (vines) reaching up to 30 m (90 ft) by climbing up trees. They can be either deciduous or evergreen, though the widely cultivated temperate species are all deciduous. Hydrangeas are famous for their large, showy flower clusters that can change color based on the acidity of the soil. In alkaline soils, flowers are pink, while in acidic soils, they become blue.

The peony or paeony is a flowering plant in the genus Paeonia, the only genus in the family Paeoniaceae. Peonies are native to Asia, Europe and Western North America. Scientists differ on the number of species that can be distinguished, ranging from 25 to 40, although the current consensus is around 33 species. Most are herbaceous perennial plants 0.5–1.5 metres (1.6–4.9 ft) tall, but some are woody shrubs 1.5–3 metres (4.9–9.8 ft) tall. They have compound, deeply lobed leaves and large, often fragrant flowers, in colors ranging from purple and pink to red, white or yellow, in late spring and early summer. Peonies are among the most popular garden plants in temperate regions and are also sold as cut flowers on a large scale.

Dahlia is a genus of bushy, tuberous, herbaceous perennial plants native to Mexico and Central America. A member of the Compositae (also known as Asteraceae) family of dicotyledonous plants, its garden relatives thus include the sunflower, daisy, chrysanthemum, and zinnia. There are 42 species of dahlia, with hybrids commonly grown as garden plants. Flower forms are variable, with one head per stem; these can be as small as 5 cm (2 in) diameter or up to 30 cm (12 in) ('dinner plate'). This great variety results from dahlias being octoploids—that is, they have eight sets of homologous chromosomes, whereas most plants have only two. Dahlias are known for their vibrant colors and complex petal arrangements.

Hibiscus is a genus of flowering plants in the mallow family, Malvaceae. The genus is quite large, comprising several hundred species that are native to warm temperate, subtropical and tropical regions throughout the world. Member species are renowned for their large, showy flowers and those species are commonly known simply as 'hibiscus', or less widely known as rose mallow. Other names include hardy hibiscus, rose of sharon, and tropical hibiscus. The genus includes both annual and perennial herbaceous plants, as well as woody shrubs and small trees. The flowers are large, conspicuous, trumpet-shaped, with five or more petals, in a range of colors including white, pink, red, orange, peach, yellow or purple.

Magnolia is a large genus of about 210 to 340 flowering plant species in the subfamily Magnolioideae of the family Magnoliaceae. The genus Magnolia is named after French botanist Pierre Magnol. Magnolias are an ancient genus. Appearing before bees did, the flowers are theorized to have evolved to encourage pollination by beetles. To avoid damage from pollinating beetles, the carpels of Magnolia flowers are extremely tough. Fossilized specimens of M. acuminata have been found dating to 20 million years ago, and of plants identifiably belonging to the Magnoliaceae dating to 95 million years ago. Another aspect of Magnolia considered ancestral is that the flower bud is enclosed in a bract rather than in sepals.

Cornus is a genus of about 30–60 species of woody plants in the family Cornaceae, commonly known as dogwoods. Most are deciduous trees or shrubs, but a few species are nearly herbaceous perennial subshrubs, and a few of the woody species are evergreen. Several species have small heads of inconspicuous flowers surrounded by an involucre of large, typically white petal-like bracts, while other species have more open clusters of small flowers. Dogwoods are found across the Northern Hemisphere and are popular in landscaping for their attractive flowers, berries, and bark. The wood of some species is extremely hard and dense, and has historically been used to make daggers, tool handles, and shuttles for weaving.

Wisteria is a genus of flowering plants in the legume family, Fabaceae (Leguminosae), that includes ten species of woody climbing bines (twining vines) that are native to China, Korea, Japan, and the Eastern United States. Some species are popular ornamental plants. An aquatic flowering plant with the common name wisteria or 'water wisteria' is in fact Hygrophila difformis, in the family Acanthaceae. Wisteria vines climb by twining their stems clockwise or counter-clockwise around any available support. They can reach heights of 20 m (66 ft) above the ground and spread 10 m (33 ft) laterally. The world's largest known wisteria is in Sierra Madre, California, measuring more than 1 acre (0.40 ha) in size and weighing 250 tons.

Rhododendron is a very large genus of about 1,024 species of woody plants in the heath family (Ericaceae), either evergreen or deciduous, and found mainly in Asia, although it is also widespread throughout the Southern Highlands of the Appalachian Mountains of North America. It is the national flower of Nepal as well as the state flower of West Virginia and Washington. Most species have showy flowers which bloom from late winter through early summer. Azaleas make up two subgenera of Rhododendron. They are distinguished from 'true' rhododendrons by having only five anthers per flower. Rhododendrons vary in size from small shrubs to large trees. They are popular ornamental plants in gardens and landscapes.

Azaleas are flowering shrubs in the genus Rhododendron, particularly the former sections Tsutsusi (evergreen) and Pentanthera (deciduous). Azaleas bloom in the spring, their flowers often lasting several weeks. Shade tolerant, they prefer living near or under trees. They are part of the family Ericaceae. Azaleas are distinguished from 'true' rhododendrons by having only five anthers per flower, whereas rhododendrons have ten or more. They are popular ornamental plants and are often used in landscaping for their vibrant and profuse blooms. There are thousands of cultivars of azaleas, with a wide range of flower colors including white, pink, red, and purple.

Lily of the valley (Convallaria majalis), sometimes written lily-of-the-valley, is a highly poisonous woodland flowering plant with sweetly scented, pendent, bell-shaped white flowers borne in sprays in spring. It is native throughout the cool temperate Northern Hemisphere in Asia and Europe. It is the only species in the genus Convallaria in the flowering plant family Asparagaceae. All parts of the plant, including the berries, are highly poisonous if ingested, containing over 38 different cardiac glycosides. Despite its toxicity, it is a popular garden plant and is often used in perfumes for its delicate fragrance. It is also a symbol of humility and purity in various cultures.

A fern (Polypodiopsida) is a member of a group of vascular plants (plants with xylem and phloem) that reproduce via spores and have neither seeds nor flowers. They differ from mosses by being vascular, i.e., having specialized tissues that conduct water and nutrients and in having life cycles in which the branched sporophyte is the dominant phase. Ferns have complex leaves called fronds, each frond being a megaphyll, which is more complex than the microphyll of a clubmoss. Most ferns are leptosporangiate ferns. They appear in the fossil record 360 million years ago in the late Devonian period, but many of the current families and species did not appear until roughly 145 million years ago in the early Cretaceous.

Tagetes is a genus of annual or perennial, mostly herbaceous plants in the sunflower family Asteraceae. They are among several groups of plants known in English as marigolds. The genus Tagetes was described by Carl Linnaeus in 1753. The plants are native to the Americas, growing naturally from the southwestern United States into Mexico and south through Central and South America. Marigolds are recorded in many cultures as being used for food, medicine, and ritual. They are very popular in gardens for their bright orange and yellow flowers and their ability to repel certain pests. The pungent scent of the leaves and flowers is a natural deterrent for many insects and soil-borne nematodes.

Narcissus is a genus of predominantly spring perennial plants in the amaryllis family, Amaryllidaceae. Various common names including daffodil, narcissus and jonquil are used to describe all or some members of the genus. Narcissus has conspicuous flowers with six petal-like tepals surmounted by a cup- or trumpet-shaped corona. The flowers are generally white or yellow (also orange or pink in garden varieties), with either uniform or contrasting coloured tepals and corona. Narcissus were well known in ancient civilisation, both medicinally and botanically, but formally described by Linnaeus in his Species Plantarum (1753). The species are native to meadows and woods in Europe, North Africa and the Western Mediterranean, with a center of diversity in the Iberian Peninsula.

Tulips (Tulipa) are a genus of spring-blooming perennial herbaceous bulbiferous geophytes (having bulbs as storage organs). The flowers are usually large, showy and brightly colored, generally red, pink, yellow, or white (usually in warm colors).
