They are primarily carnivorous and eat a wide variety of small animals, including insects, rodents, birds, and reptiles. They are famous for their ability to kill venomous snakes. They are often seen in pairs or small family groups. In ancient Egypt, they were sometimes considered sacred animals and were often depicted in art.

The fennec fox (Vulpes zerda) is a small crepuscular fox native to the deserts of North Africa, ranging from the Western Sahara to the Sinai Peninsula. Its most distinctive feature is its unusually large ears, which serve to dissipate heat and help it hear prey moving underground. The fennec is the smallest species of canid. Its coat, ears, and kidney functions have adapted to high-temperature, low-water, desert environments. Also, its paws are covered with thick fur to protect them from the hot sand. Its fur is a pale, sand color that provides excellent camouflage. Fennec foxes are social animals and live in small groups. They are omnivorous and eat a variety of insects, small mammals, and plants. They have become popular as exotic pets in some parts of the world.

The caracal (Caracal caracal) is a medium-sized wild cat native to Africa, the Middle East, Central Asia, and India. it is characterised by a robust build, long legs, a short face, long tufted ears, and long canine teeth. Its coat is uniformly reddish-tan or sandy, while the ventral parts are lighter with small reddish markings. Reaching 40–50 cm (16–20 in) at the shoulder, it is a powerful and agile predator. The caracal is famous for its ability to leap high into the air to catch birds in flight. It is primarily a nocturnal and solitary animal, although it may be active during the day in some areas. its diet includes small mammals, birds, and occasionally larger ungulates. The caracal is often considered highly elusive and difficult to spot in the wild.

The serval (Leptailurus serval) is a slender, medium-sized wild cat native to Africa. It is the only member of the genus Leptailurus. It is characterised by a small head, large ears, a golden-yellow to buff coat spotted and striped with black, and a short, black-tipped tail. The serval has the longest legs of any cat relative to its body size, which help it move quickly through tall grass and leap high into the air. It is a highly specialized predator that primarily hunts small mammals, especially rodents, using its incredible hearing to locate them moving in the grass. It can also catch birds and frogs. Servals are primarily found in savannahs and wetlands near water. They are solitary and territorial animals and are most active at dawn and dusk.

The bharal (Pseudois nayaur), also known as the Himalayan blue sheep or naur, is a caprid native to the high Himalayas of India, Nepal, Bhutan, Tibet, and Pakistan. Despite its name, it is thought to be more closely related to goats than to sheep. Bharal are medium-sized goats with a slate-blue or greyish-brown coat that provides excellent camouflage against the rocky slopes where they live. The males have large, smooth, backward-curving horns, while the female's horns are much shorter and straighter. Bharal are highly agile and are typical inhabitants of steep, rocky terrain at high altitudes, ranging from 3,000 to 5,000 meters. They are a primary prey source for the snow leopard. They live in social groups and are primarily grazers and browsers.

The wild yak (Bos mutus) is a large, long-haired bovid found throughout the Himalayan region of South Central Asia, the Tibetan Plateau and as far north as Mongolia and Russia. It is the ancestor of the domestic yak (Bos grunniens). Wild yaks are massive animals, with adult males weighing up to 1,000 kg (2,200 lb). They have a thick, shaggy coat, usually black or dark brown, that protects them from the extreme cold of their high-altitude environment. They also have a large, humped shoulder and massive, curved horns. Wild yaks are found in treeless alpine meadows and cold deserts at elevations from 3,000 to 5,500 meters. They are social animals and often form large herds. They are primarily grazers and browsers, eating a variety of grasses and other alpine plants. They are increasingly threatened by habitat loss and competition with domestic livestock.

The red deer (Cervus elaphus) is one of the largest deer species. The red deer inhabits most of Europe, the Caucasus Mountains region, Asia Minor, Iran, parts of western Asia, and central Asia. It also inhabits the Atlas Mountains region between Morocco and Tunisia in northwestern Africa, being the only species of deer to inhabit Africa. Red deer have been introduced to other areas, including Australia, New Zealand, the United States, Canada, Peru, Uruguay, Chile and Argentina. They are known for their reddish-brown coat and their impressive, many-tined antlers in the males (stags). Red deer are social animals and often form large herds. They are primary grazers and browsers, eating a variety of grasses and other plants. They have long been hunted for their meat and antlers and have deep cultural significance in many societies.

The fallow deer (Dama dama) is a species of ruminant mammal belonging to the family Cervidae. It is native to Europe and western Asia, but has been introduced to many other parts of the world, including North America, Australasia, and South Africa. Fallow deer are easily recognized by their spotted coats, which are most prominent in the summer, and their spectacular palmate (hand-shaped) antlers in the males. They come in a variety of color morphs, including white, black, and the typical chestnut with white spots. Fallow deer are social animals and are often seen in small groups. They are primary grazers and browsers and are often found in woodlands and parks. They have been popular as ornamental deer in estates and parks for centuries.

The chamois (Rupicapra rupicapra) is a species of goat-antelope native to mountains in Europe, from west to east, including the Cantabrian mountains, the Pyrenees, the Alps, the Apennines, the Dinarides, the Tatra and the Balkan mountains, the Rila – Rhodope massif, and the Caucasus and the Pontic Mountains. It has also been introduced to the South Island of New Zealand. The chamois is a highly agile and sure-footed climber, perfectly adapted for life in steep, rocky terrain at high altitudes. It has a brownish coat with a dark dorsal stripe and two dark facial stripes. Both males and females have short, sharp, hook-like black horns. Chamois live in small social groups and are primarily browsers and grazers. They are known for their incredible speed and jumping ability on difficult terrain.

The Alpine ibex (Capra ibex), also known as the steinbock or bouquetin, is a species of wild goat that lives in the mountains of the European Alps. It is a sexually dimorphic species with larger males who carry large, curved horns. The coat colour is typically brownish-grey. Ibex are highly agile and are expert climbers, capable of moving across extremely steep and rocky slopes with ease. They are found at high altitudes, ranging from 1,800 to 3,300 meters, and are often seen above the tree line. Ibex were once nearly hunted to extinction, but successful reintroduction programs have helped the population recover. They are social animals and live in groups, often segregated by sex. They are primarily browsers and grazers, eating a variety of alpine plants.

The wild boar (Sus scrofa), also known as the wild swine, common wild pig, or simply wild pig, is a suid native to much of Eurasia and North Africa, and has been introduced to much of the Americas and Oceania. The species is now one of the most wide-ranging mammals in the world, as well as the most widespread suiform. Its wide range, high numbers, and adaptability mean that it is classed as Least Concern by the IUCN and it has become an invasive species in part of its introduced range. Wild boars are powerful animals with a thick, bristly coat and large tusks, which are modified canine teeth used for defense and digging. They are highly social and live in groups called sounders. They are omnivorous and will eat almost anything, including roots, tubers, fruit, and small animals.

The raccoon (Procyon lotor), sometimes spelled racoon, also known as the common raccoon, North American raccoon, northern raccoon, or colloquially as coon, is a medium-sized mammal native to North America. The raccoon is the largest of the procyonid family, having a body length of 40 to 70 cm (16 to 28 in) and a body weight of 5 to 26 kg (11 to 57 lb). Its grayish coat mostly consists of dense underfur which insulates it against cold weather. Two of the raccoon's most distinctive features are its extremely dexterous front paws and its facial mask, which are themes in the mythologies of 여러 indigenous peoples of the Americas. Raccoons are noted for their intelligence, with studies showing that they are able to remember the solution to tasks for up to three years. They are highly adaptable and are found in a variety of habitats, including forests and urban areas.

The Virginia opossum (Didelphis virginiana), commonly known as the North American opossum, is a marsupial found in North America. It is the only marsupial found in North America north of Mexico. The Virginia opossum is a medium-sized animal, about the size of a domestic cat. It is easily recognized by its pointed snout, large ears, and long, prehensile, hairless tail. Its fur is typically greyish with a white face. Opossums are famous for their defensive behavior of 'playing possum', where they will feign death when threatened by a predator. They are highly adaptable and are found in a variety of habitats, including forests and suburban areas. They are opportunistic omnivores and eat a variety of insects, rodents, fruit, and carrion. They are also known for their resistance to many types of snake venom.

The North American beaver (Castor canadensis) is one of two extant species of beavers. It is native to North America and has been introduced to Patagonia and several countries in Europe. After the capybara, it is the second-largest rodent in the world. Beavers are famous for their incredible ability to transform their environment by building dams, canals, and lodges. These structures create wetlands that provide habitat for a huge variety of other wildlife. Beavers have a thick, waterproof coat, webbed hind feet, and a large, flat, scaly tail used for swimming and balance. They are primarily herbivorous and eat a variety of aquatic plants, as well as the bark and wood of trees such as willow and aspen. They have large, orange-colored front teeth that grow continuously and are used for gnawing on wood.

The North American river otter (Lontra canadensis), also known as the northern river otter or the common otter, is a semiaquatic mammal endemic to the North American continent found in and along its waterways and coasts. An adult North American river otter can weigh between 5 and 14 kg (11 and 31 lb). The river otter is protected and insulated by a thick, water-repellent coat of fur. They have streamlined bodies, webbed feet, and long, powerful tails, making them excellent swimmers. They are highly social and are often seen playing and sliding down muddy banks. Their diet consists primarily of fish, but they also eat crustaceans, amphibians, and small mammals. They are sensitive to water pollution and are considered an indicator of a healthy aquatic ecosystem.

The striped skunk (Mephitis mephitis) is a skunk of the genus Mephitis that is native to North America. It is the most common skunk in the world and is found across most of the continental United States and southern Canada. Striped skunks are easily recognized by their black fur with two bold white stripes that run from the head to the tail. They are famous for their spectacular defensive ability to spray a pungent and irritating fluid from glands near their tail when threatened. This spray is powerful enough to deter even large predators. They are primarily nocturnal and are opportunistic omnivores, eating a variety of insects, rodents, fruit, and carrion. They are highly adaptable and are found in a variety of habitats, including forests, grasslands, and suburban areas.

The American mink (Neovison vison) is a semiaquatic species of mustelid native to North America, though human intervention has expanded its range to many parts of Europe, Asia, and South America. It is the only member of the genus Neovison. The American mink is a slender, medium-sized carnivore, with a body length of 30 to 50 cm (12 to 20 in) and a long, bushy tail. Its fur is traditionally brown, but is now bred in a variety of colors for the fur trade. Minks are highly adapted for an aquatic lifestyle and are excellent swimmers. They are primarily carnivorous and hunt a variety of prey, including fish, frogs, rodents, and birds. They are often found near rivers, lakes, and marshes. They are solitary and territorial animals and are considered a highly efficient and successful predator.

The fisher (Pekania pennanti) is a small carnivorous mammal native to North America. It is a member of the mustelid family (Mustelidae) and is the only living species in the genus Pekania. Despite its name, the fisher rarely eats fish; the name instead comes from its resemblance to the European polecat, which was known as the 'fitch'. The fisher is a slender, medium-sized carnivore, with a dark brown to black coat and a long, bushy tail. It is a highly agile and successful predator, capable of climbing trees with great speed. It is one of the few predators capable of killing porcupines, which it does by attacking their vulnerable head. Fishers are primarily found in coniferous and mixed forests and are solitary and elusive animals.

The American marten (Martes americana), also known as the pine marten, is a North American mammal, a member of the family Mustelidae. The species is sometimes referred to as the American pine marten. The Newfoundland pine marten (Martes americana atrata) is a threatened subspecies of the American marten found only on the island of Newfoundland. The American marten is a slender, medium-sized carnivore, with a soft, silky coat ranging from light brown to black and a characteristic orange or yellow throat patch. They are highly arboreal and are excellent climbers, moving with great speed through the trees. They are primarily carnivorous and hunt a variety of small mammals, birds, and insects. They are found in coniferous and mixed forests and are solitary and elusive animals.

The stoat (Mustela erminea), also known as the short-tailed weasel or simply the weasel in Ireland, is a species of mustelid native to Eurasia and North America. It is distinguished from the least weasel by its larger size and its longer tail with a prominent black tip. In northern parts of its range, the stoat's coat turns from brown to white in the winter, although the black tip on the tail remains. In this white state, it is known as an ermine. Stoats are small, agile, and highly successful predators, capable of killing prey much larger than themselves, such as rabbits. They are primarily found in a variety of habitats, including woodlands, grasslands, and marshes. They are solitary and territorial animals and are known for their energetic and focused hunting style.

The least weasel (Mustela nivalis), also known as the common weasel, little weasel, or simply weasel, is the smallest member of the genus Mustela, the family Mustelidae, and the order Carnivora. It is native to Eurasia, North America, and North Africa and has been introduced to New Zealand, Australia, and several other countries. Despite its tiny size, the least weasel is a ferocious and efficient predator, capable of killing prey twice its size. It has a long, slender body that allows it to follow rodents into their burrows. Its fur is typically brown above and white below. Like the stoat, in northern regions, it may turn white in the winter. They are found in a variety of habitats, including woodlands, grasslands, and agricultural areas. They are highly active and have a very high metabolic rate.

The bobcat (Lynx rufus) is a medium-sized North American cat that appears from southern Canada to central Mexico, including most of the contiguous United States. It is a resilient and adaptable predator that inhabits a variety of environments, including forests, semi-deserts, urban edges, forest edges, and swampland environments. The bobcat is about twice as large as a domestic cat and is characterized by its short (bobbed) tail, tufted ears, and a ruff of hair on its cheeks. Its coat is uniformly reddish-tan or greyish, with prominent black spots and bars. Bobcats are primarily nocturnal and solitary animals and are ambush predators. Their diet includes a variety of prey, from insects and rodents to deer. They are highly territorial and use a variety of calls to communicate.

The collared peccary (Pecari tajacu), also known as the javelina or musk hog, is a mammal in the family Tayassuidae native to the Americas. Peccaries were originally classified in the family Suidae, along with pigs, but they are now placed in their own family. The collared peccary is native to the Southwestern United States, through Mexico and Central America, and as far south as northern Argentina in South America. They have a pig-like appearance, with a thick coat of bristly grey or black hair and a faint white 'collar' around their neck. Peccaries are highly social and live in groups called squadrons, which can number from a few individuals to over 50. They are opportunists and eat a variety of plants, roots, tubers, and occasionally small animals. They are known for their strong scent, which they use to communicate with their group.

The northern tamandua (Tamandua mexicana) is a species of anteater in the family Myrmecophagidae native to Central and northern South America. It is a medium-sized anteater, about the size of a domestic cat. It is characterized by its elongated snout, long, curved claws, and its distinctive, prehensile tail which it uses for climbing. Its fur is typically a light yellow or tan with a pale, V-shaped 'vest' on its back. Northern tamanduas are primarily arboreal and are excellent climbers, spending most of their time in the trees hunting for ants and termites. They have no teeth and use their long, sticky tongue to extract their prey from mounds and crevices. They are solitary and primarily nocturnal animals. They are often found in tropical and subtropical forests.

Spider monkeys are New World monkeys belonging to the genus Ateles, part of the subfamily Atelinae, family Atelidae. Like other atelines, they are found in tropical forests of Central and South America, from southern Mexico to Brazil. The genus contains seven species, all of which are under threat; the black-headed spider monkey and brown spider monkey are critically endangered. Spider monkeys have long, extremely thin limbs and highly prehensile tails, which they use as a fifth limb for climbing and swinging through the trees. They are among the most agile of New World monkeys and can move with great speed through the canopy. They are primary frugivores and play a vital role in seed dispersal. They live in large social groups and are known for their loud and complex vocalizations.

Howler monkeys (genus Alouatta) are among the largest of the New World monkeys. They are famous for their incredibly loud vocalizations, which are among the loudest of any land animal. These howls can be heard from a distance of up to 5 kilometers (3 miles) and are used to establish territory and communicate between groups. There are 15 recognized species of howler monkeys, native to the tropical forests of Central and South America. They have long, thick fur and highly prehensile tails. Unlike most New World monkeys, they are primarily folivores, eating a variety of leaves, although they also eat some fruit. Howler monkeys have a specialized bone in their throat, called the hyoid bone, that allows them to produce their powerful calls. They live in small, stable social groups.

Marmosets are small New World monkeys of the family Callitrichidae. There are 22 recognized species of marmosets, divided into four genera. They are native to South America, with the greatest diversity being found in the Amazon rainforest. Marmosets are tiny, even among monkeys, and some species are among the smallest primates in the world. They have soft, silky fur and long tails. One of their most distinctive features is that they have claws instead of nails on most of their digits, which allow them to cling to and move quickly through the branches of trees. Marmosets are highly social and live in small groups that are usually led by a dominant breeding pair. They are omnivorous and eat a variety of insects, fruit, and exudates like tree sap and gum.

The African civet (Civettictis civetta) is a large viverrid native to sub-Saharan Africa. It is the only member of the genus Civettictis. It is classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List as it has a wide range and a large stable population. The African civet is a stocky and short-legged animal, with a body length of 60 to 90 cm (24 to 35 in) and a long, bushy tail. Its fur is typically greyish with a complex pattern of black spots and bars, providing excellent camouflage in the forest undergrowth. African civets are primarily nocturnal and solitary animals. They are opportunistic omnivores and eat a variety of insects, rodents, birds, fruit, and carrion. They have a specialized gland near their tail that secretes a pungent substance called civetone, which has been used in perfumes for centuries.

Genets are members of the genus Genetta in the family Viverridae. There are approximately 14 to 17 species of genets, native to Africa and southern Europe. Genets are slender, cat-like animals with a long, ringed tail and a spotted coat. They have large ears and retractable claws that allow them to climb trees with great agility. Genets are primarily nocturnal and are efficient and successful predators. They hunt a variety of small animals, including rodents, birds, and insects. Despite their cat-like appearance, they are more closely related to mongooses and civets than to true cats. They are found in a variety of habitats, including woodlands, savannahs, and rocky areas. They are mostly solitary and territorial animals and are known for their elusive and secretive nature.

The Sunda pangolin (Manis javanica), also known as the Malayan or Javan pangolin, is a species of pangolin native to Southeast Asia. It is found in Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam. It is a large, scaly mammal that is both terrestrial and arboreal. Like all pangolins, it is covered in large, overlapping scales made of keratin. When threatened, it rolls into a tight ball for protection. The Sunda pangolin is primarily nocturnal and insectivorous, feeding on ants and termites which it dig out with its powerful claws and extracts with its long, sticky tongue. It has no teeth. It is listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List as it is the most heavily trafficked mammal in the world, primarily for its meat and scales, which are used in traditional medicine.

The wild water buffalo (Bubalus arnee), also called Asian buffalo and Asiatic buffalo, is a large bovid native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia. It is the ancestor of the domestic water buffalo (Bubalus bubalis). Wild water buffalo are massive animals, with adult males weighing up to 1,200 kg (2,600 lb). They have a thick, sparse coat and massive, crescent-shaped horns that can reach over 1.5 meters in length. They are highly aquatic and are found in wet grasslands, swamps, and rivers. They are social animals and often form large herds. They are primary grazers, eating a variety of aquatic and terrestrial grasses. They are listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to habitat loss and competition with domestic livestock. Only a few thousand wild water buffalo remain in scattered populations.

The banteng (Bos javanicus), also known as tembadau, is a species of wild cattle native to Southeast Asia. It is one of the three extant species of wild cattle in the region, along with the gaur and the kouprey. The banteng is a large and powerfully built animal, with adult males weighing up to 900 kg (2,000 lb). They have a reddish-brown to black coat, with white patches on their legs and buttocks. Both males and females have curved horns, but the male's horns are much larger. Banteng are found in open forests and savannahs and are social animals that live in small herds. They are primarily grazers and browsers, eating a variety of grasses and other plants. They are listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to poaching and habitat loss.

The anoa is a subgenus of the Bubalus genus, comprising two species endemic to the island of Sulawesi in Indonesia: the mountain anoa (Bubalus quarlesi) and the lowland anoa (Bubalus depressicornis). Both species are the smallest wild cattle in the world. They inhabit undisturbed rainforests and are characterized by their stocky build, short legs, and small, straight horns. The lowland anoa is slightly larger than the mountain anoa. Both species are elusive and solitary, but they are sometimes seen in pairs. They are primary browsers, eating a variety of forest plants. Both species are listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to habitat loss and hunting.

The Malayan tapir (Tapirus indicus), also called the Asian tapir, is the largest of the four species of tapir and the only one native to Asia. It is found in Southeast Asia, including Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, and the island of Sumatra in Indonesia. It is easily recognized by its distinctive black-and-white 'saddle' pattern, which provides excellent camouflage in the shadows of the forest floor. Malayan tapirs are large, stocky animals with a short, flexible trunk used for plucking leaves and fruit. They are primarily nocturnal and solitary. They are excellent swimmers and are often found near water. They are listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to habitat loss and fragmentation.

The saola (Pseudoryx nghetinhensis), also known as the Vu Quang ox, spindlehorn, or Asian unicorn, is one of the world's rarest large mammals, a forest-dwelling bovine found only in the Annamite Range of Vietnam and Laos. It was only discovered in 1992, making it one of the most spectacular zoological discoveries of the 20th century. The saola is characterized by its long, straight, parallel horns and the white markings on its face. It is an elusive and secretive animal, and very little is known about its behavior and population in the wild. It is listed as Critically Endangered on the IUCN Red List, and it is thought that only a few dozen individuals remain. It is a symbol of biodiversity and the need for urgent conservation efforts.

The American pika (Ochotona princeps) is a species of pika native to the mountains of western North America. It is a small, herbivorous mammal, a member of the order Lagomorpha, which also includes rabbits and hares. American pikas are about the size of a large hamster and have round ears and no visible tail. They are perfectly adapted for life in cold, alpine environments, typically living in rock slides or talus slopes. They are famous for their 'haystacking' behavior, where they gather and dry grasses and flowers during the summer to eat during the long winter. They are very sensitive to high temperatures and are often considered an indicator species for climate change.

The groundhog (Marmota monax), also known as the woodchuck, is a rodent of the family Sciuridae, belonging to the group of large ground squirrels known as marmots. It is found across much of North America, from the eastern United States through Canada and into Alaska. Unlike most other marmots, which live in rocky and mountainous areas, the groundhog is primarily a lowland animal. It is a large, stocky rodent with a thick, reddish-brown coat and power claws for digging complex underground burrows. Groundhogs are famous for their hibernation and for the 'Groundhog Day' tradition in North America. They are primarily herbivorous and eat a variety of grasses, clover, and garden vegetables.

Prairie dogs (genus Cynomys) are herbivorous burrowing rodents native to the grasslands of North America. The five species are: black-tailed, white-tailed, Gunnison's, Utah, and Mexican prairie dogs. Despite their name, they are not dogs but are members of the squirrel family Sciuridae. Prairie dogs are highly social animals and live in large, complex colonies called 'towns'. These towns can cover hundreds of acres and provide habitat for many other species. They are famous for their complex communication system, which involves a variety of barks and chirps to alert the colony of predators. They are a classic keystone species, as their burrowing and grazing have a significant impact on the grassland ecosystem.

The eastern chipmunk (Tamias striatus) is a chipmunk species found in eastern North America. It is the only living member of the chipmunk subgenus Tamias. It is found in deciduous and mixed forests as well as suburban gardens. Eastern chipmunks are easily recognized by their reddish-brown fur with five dark stripes on their back. They have large cheek pouches that they use to carry food, especially nuts and seeds, to their underground burrows. They are active during the day and are often seen scurrying across the forest floor. While they do not truly hibernate, they spend much of the winter in their burrows, waking up periodically to eat from their food caches. They are an important food source for many predators.

The red squirrel or Eurasian red squirrel (Sciurus vulgaris) is a species of tree squirrel in the genus Sciurus common throughout Eurasia. The red squirrel is an arboreal, primarily herbivorous rodent. In Great Britain and Ireland, numbers have decreased drastically in recent years, partly because of the introduction of the eastern grey squirrel from North America, which competes for food and carries a virus that is fatal to the red squirrel. Red squirrels are characterized by their vibrant reddish-brown coat and their distinctive tufted ears in the winter. They are highly agile and spend most of their time in the trees. They are famous for caching nuts and seeds to eat during the winter, which plays a vital role in forest regeneration through seed dispersal.

The southern flying squirrel (Glaucomys volans) is one of three species of the genus Glaucomys, the only flying squirrels native to North America. It is found in deciduous and mixed forests throughout the eastern United States and parts of southeastern Canada. The southern flying squirrel does not actually fly, but rather glides through the air using a thin membrane of skin, called a patagium, that extends from its wrists to its ankles. They are primarily nocturnal and have large, dark eyes adapted for low-light conditions. They are highly social and often nest together in groups, especially in the winter to stay warm. They are omnivorous and eat a variety of nuts, seeds, fruit, and insects.

The kowari (Dasyuroides byrnei), also known as the brush-tailed marsupial rat, Byrne's crest-tailed marsupial rat, or Kayerat, is a small carnivorous marsupial native to the gibber deserts of central Australia. It is the only member of the genus Dasyuroides. The kowari is about the size of a small rat and has a greyish-brown coat with a distinctive black-tufted tail. It is a highly efficient and successful predator, hunting a variety of small mammals, reptiles, and insects in its harsh desert environment. Kowaris are primarily nocturnal and spend the day in underground burrows. They are listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List due to habitat loss and predation by introduced species like feral cats and foxes.

The greater bilby (Macrotis lagotis), often referred to simply as the bilby, is an Australian species of nocturnal omnivorous marsupial in the order Peramelemorphia. The name 'bilby' is a loanword from the Yuwaalaraay Aboriginal language of northern New South Wales, meaning 'long-nosed rat'. The bilby is characterized by its long, rabbit-like ears, its pointed snout, and its soft, blue-grey fur with a black and white tail. It is an expert burrower and uses its strong claws to dig complex underground systems. Bilbies have become a symbol of Australian wildlife conservation, and the 'Easter Bilby' is a popular alternative to the Easter Bunny in Australia. They are increasingly threatened by habitat loss and predation by introduced species.

The Philippine tarsier (Carlito syrichta), known locally as the mawmag in Cebuano and magô in Waray, is a species of tarsier endemic to the Philippines. It is found in the southeastern part of the archipelago, particularly on the islands of Bohol, Samar, Leyte and Mindanao. It is one of the smallest primates in the world, with a body length of about 8.5 to 16 cm (3.3 to 6.3 in). The Philippine tarsier is famous for its enormous eyes, which are larger than its brain and cannot move in their sockets; instead, the tarsier can rotate its head 180 degrees. They are primarily nocturnal and insectivorous, and are capable of leaping long distances between trees. They are highly sensitive to stress and do not survive well in captivity.

Slow lorises are a group of several species of nocturnal strepsirrhine primates which make up the genus Nycticebus. Found in Southeast Asia and bordering areas, they range from Bangladesh and Northeast India in the west to the Philippines in the east, and from the Yunnan province in China in the north to the island of Java in the south. Slow lorises are characterized by their round heads, large eyes, and slow, deliberate movements. They are among the few venomous mammals; they have a specialized gland on their arm that produces a toxin, which they lick and then use in their bite for defense. They have soft, thick fur that varies in color. Slow lorises are primarily nocturnal and omnivorous, eating a variety of insects, fruit, and exudates. They are increasingly threatened by the illegal pet trade and habitat loss.
